title: Longest Prefix Matching Any Suffix
description: Find longest prefix appearing again as substring

examples.explanation:
String: "ababa", n=5
Check L=4: prefix="abab", compare with positions i=1: compare "baba" vs "abab" -> mismatch at j=0 (b vs a), no match
Check L=3: prefix="aba", compare with i=1: "bab" vs "aba" -> mismatch at j=0 (b vs a)
                         compare with i=2: "aba" vs "aba" -> all characters match: a==a, b==b, a==a, match found
Return L=3

String: "aaaa", n=4
Check L=3: prefix="aaa", compare with i=1: "aaa" vs "aaa" -> all match: a==a, a==a, a==a, match found
Return L=3

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Check every possible prefix against every possible substring position in the string. For each prefix length, scan through the string to see if that prefix appears elsewhere as a substring.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n³) Solution for Longest Prefix Matching Any Suffix
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking every prefix against every substring position in the string.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach solves the problem by systematically examining all possible prefixes of the string and checking if each appears elsewhere as a substring. The intuition is straightforward: we want the longest prefix that appears again somewhere in the string, so we check prefixes from longest to shortest, stopping at the first match. For each prefix length L (from n-1 down to 1), we extract the prefix substring s[0:L] and then scan through the string starting from position 1 to n-L to see if this prefix appears anywhere else. The scanning involves comparing characters one by one. The algorithm has three nested loops: outer loop for prefix length, middle loop for starting position of comparison, and inner loop for character-by-character comparison, resulting in O(n³) time complexity. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by using the substring matching logic as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, not handling edge cases like empty strings or strings with no matching prefix, and inefficient character-by-character comparison instead of using built-in substring functions. The brute force approach, while simple, is inefficient for large strings but serves as a foundational understanding before optimizing.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize result = 0
2. For L from n-1 down to 1 (prefix length):
   a. Extract prefix = s[0:L]
   b. For i from 1 to n-L (starting positions for comparison):
      i. Set match = True
      ii. For j from 0 to L-1 (character comparison):
          - If s[i+j] != prefix[j], set match = False and break
      iii. If match is True, set result = L and return result
3. Return result (0 if no match found)

Time analysis: O(n³) worst-case when no match exists, as all three loops run fully. Space analysis: O(1) for variables.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def longest_prefix_match(s):
    n = len(s)
    for L in range(n-1, 0, -1):
        prefix = s[:L]
        for i in range(1, n-L+1):
            match = True
            for j in range(L):
                if s[i+j] != prefix[j]:
                    match = False
                    break
            if match:
                return L
    return 0

examples.trace:
String: "ababa", n=5
Check L=4: prefix="abab", compare with positions i=1: compare "baba" vs "abab" -> mismatch at j=0 (b vs a), no match
Check L=3: prefix="aba", compare with i=1: "bab" vs "aba" -> mismatch at j=0 (b vs a)
                         compare with i=2: "aba" vs "aba" -> all characters match: a==a, b==b, a==a, match found
Return L=3

String: "aaaa", n=4
Check L=3: prefix="aaa", compare with i=1: "aaa" vs "aaa" -> all match: a==a, a==a, a==a, match found
Return L=3

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Substring Matching for a Given Prefix
ladders.desc: Check if a specific prefix appears as a substring elsewhere in the string.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for determining whether a given prefix string appears anywhere in the main string beyond its original position at the start. The intuition is that we need to scan through the string, starting from position 1 (to avoid matching the prefix with itself at position 0), and compare the prefix character-by-character with substrings of equal length. This is essentially a simplified string search problem. The algorithm takes the input string s and a prefix length L, extracts the prefix substring s[0:L], then iterates through possible starting positions i from 1 to n-L. For each position, it compares characters one by one; if all match, it returns True. If no match is found after checking all positions, it returns False. Time complexity is O(n*L) in the worst case, as we might compare up to L characters for each of up to n-L positions. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each prefix length in the outer loop. Common mistakes include incorrect loop ranges (e.g., missing the last possible starting position), not handling edge cases like L=0 or L>n, and inefficient comparison logic. The sub-routine demonstrates basic string manipulation and comparison techniques.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: string s, prefix length L
2. n = len(s)
3. If L <= 0 or L >= n, return False (no valid match possible)
4. prefix = s[0:L]
5. For i from 1 to n-L:
   a. Set match = True
   b. For j from 0 to L-1:
      - If s[i+j] != prefix[j], set match = False and break
   c. If match is True, return True
6. Return False

Time analysis: O((n-L)*L) ≤ O(n²) in worst case. Space analysis: O(1).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def prefix_appears_elsewhere(s, L):
    n = len(s)
    if L <= 0 or L >= n:
        return False
    prefix = s[:L]
    for i in range(1, n-L+1):
        match = True
        for j in range(L):
            if s[i+j] != prefix[j]:
                match = False
                break
        if match:
            return True
    return False

examples.trace:
s="ababa", n=5, L=3
prefix="aba"
Check i=1: compare s[1:4]="bab" with "aba" -> j=0: b vs a mismatch, break
Check i=2: compare s[2:5]="aba" with "aba" -> j=0: a==a, j=1: b==b, j=2: a==a, all match
Return True

s="abc", n=3, L=2
prefix="ab"
Check i=1: compare s[1:3]="bc" with "ab" -> j=0: b vs a mismatch, break
No more i to check (n-L=1)
Return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character-by-Character String Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two strings of equal length to check if they are identical.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves comparing two strings character by character to determine if they are exactly equal. The intuition is simple: two strings are equal if and only if they have the same length and every corresponding character matches. This is a fundamental operation in string processing and is used extensively in the substring matching routine. The algorithm takes two strings, str1 and str2, and first checks if their lengths are equal; if not, it returns False immediately. Then, it iterates through each index from 0 to length-1, comparing characters at each position. If any mismatch is found, it returns False; if all characters match, it returns True. Time complexity is O(L) where L is the length of the strings, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L1 by being the inner comparison step in the substring matching. Common mistakes include not checking length equality first (which can lead to index errors or incorrect results), using inefficient comparison methods, or not handling empty strings properly. This logic is essential for understanding how string equality works at a low level, which is crucial for algorithm design.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: strings str1 and str2
2. If len(str1) != len(str2), return False
3. For i from 0 to len(str1)-1:
   a. If str1[i] != str2[i], return False
4. Return True

Time analysis: O(L) where L is the length of the strings. Space analysis: O(1).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def strings_equal(str1, str2):
    if len(str1) != len(str2):
        return False
    for i in range(len(str1)):
        if str1[i] != str2[i]:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace:
len(str1)=3, len(str2)=3, equal
Check i=0: 'a' == 'a', match
Check i=1: 'b' == 'b', match
Check i=2: 'a' == 'a', match
All characters match, return True

len(str1)=3, len(str2)=3, equal
Check i=0: 'a' == 'a', match
Check i=1: 'b' == 'b', match
Check i=2: 'c' != 'd', mismatch, return False

ladders.title: Building Block — Extracting Substrings from a String
ladders.desc: Retrieve a contiguous sequence of characters from a string given start and end indices.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves extracting a substring from a string, which is a fundamental operation in string manipulation. The intuition is that a substring is defined by a start index and a length (or end index), and it consists of all characters from the start index up to but not including the end index. In the context of the problem, we need to extract prefixes (substrings starting at index 0) and compare them with other substrings. The algorithm takes a string s, a start index start, and a length L, then returns a new string containing characters from s[start] to s[start+L-1]. It must handle edge cases such as invalid indices (e.g., start out of bounds or L too large). Time complexity is O(L) as we need to copy L characters, and space complexity is O(L) for the new substring. This feeds into L2 by providing the strings to compare. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in index calculations, not checking for valid indices, and inefficient extraction methods. Understanding substring extraction is crucial for working with strings in algorithms, as it allows us to isolate parts of the string for processing.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: string s, start index start, length L
2. n = len(s)
3. If start < 0 or start >= n or L <= 0 or start+L > n, return empty string or handle error
4. Initialize result as empty string
5. For i from start to start+L-1:
   a. Append s[i] to result
6. Return result

Time analysis: O(L) for copying characters. Space analysis: O(L) for the new string.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def extract_substring(s, start, L):
    n = len(s)
    if start < 0 or start >= n or L <= 0 or start+L > n:
        return ""
    result = ""
    for i in range(start, start+L):
        result += s[i]
    return result

examples.trace:
s="ababa", n=5, start=0, L=3
Check indices: start=0 valid, L=3 valid since 0+3=5 ≤ n=5
Initialize result=""
i=0: s[0]='a', result="a"
i=1: s[1]='b', result="ab"
i=2: s[2]='a', result="aba"
Return "aba"

s="abc", n=3, start=1, L=2
Check indices: start=1 valid, L=2 valid since 1+2=3 ≤ n=3
Initialize result=""
i=1: s[1]='b', result="b"
i=2: s[2]='c', result="bc"
Return "bc"

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Characters in a String by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve a single character from a string using its position (index).
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves accessing a specific character in a string by its index, which is fundamental to all string processing. The intuition is that a string can be thought of as an array of characters, where each character has a position starting from 0 for the first character, 1 for the second, and so on. To access a character, we use the index to retrieve it directly. In programming, this is typically done with square brackets, e.g., s[i] returns the character at index i. The algorithm is straightforward: given a string s and an index i, return s[i] if i is within the valid range (0 to len(s)-1); otherwise, handle an error (e.g., return a default value or throw an exception). Time complexity is O(1) for direct access, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 by being used to build substrings character by character. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in some contexts but not in programming), not checking for index bounds leading to errors, and confusing characters with their string representations. Mastering this operation is essential for beginners, as it forms the basis for more complex string manipulations.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: string s, index i
2. n = len(s)
3. If i < 0 or i >= n, return an error or special value (e.g., None)
4. Return s[i]

Time analysis: O(1). Space analysis: O(1).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def get_char(s, i):
    n = len(s)
    if i < 0 or i >= n:
        return None  # or raise an error
    return s[i]

examples.trace:
s="ababa", n=5, i=2
Check i: 2 >=0 and 2 <5, valid
Return s[2] = 'a'

s="abc", n=3, i=0
Check i: 0 >=0 and 0 <3, valid
Return s[0] = 'a'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Sequences of Characters
ladders.desc: Learn what a string is: an ordered collection of characters.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation concept introduces strings as fundamental data types in programming, representing sequences of characters. The intuition is that a string is like a word or text, made up of individual letters, digits, or symbols arranged in a specific order. For example, the string "hello" consists of characters 'h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o' in that order. Strings are immutable in many languages, meaning once created, their contents cannot be changed directly. Key properties include length (number of characters), indexing (accessing characters by position starting from 0), and the ability to perform operations like concatenation (joining strings) or comparison. In the context of the problem, understanding that a string has a prefix (first few characters) and can contain substrings (contiguous sequences) is crucial. This feeds into L4 by providing the basic structure for character access. Common mistakes for beginners include confusing strings with lists or other data types, not understanding zero-based indexing, or mishandling special characters. By grasping this concept, learners can build up to more complex string algorithms.

Step-by-step explanation:
1. A string is defined as a sequence of characters, e.g., "abc" has characters 'a', 'b', 'c'.
2. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains; "abc" has length 3.
3. Characters can be accessed by index: in "abc", index 0 is 'a', index 1 is 'b', index 2 is 'c'.
4. Strings can be empty ("") with length 0.
5. Operations include checking equality ("ab" == "ab" is True), concatenation ("a" + "b" = "ab"), and substring extraction.

Time analysis: Not applicable for this conceptual level. Space analysis: Strings occupy memory proportional to their length.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
# Defining strings
s1 = "hello"
s2 = "world"
# Getting length
length = len(s1)  # 5
# Accessing character
char = s1[0]  # 'h'
# Concatenation
result = s1 + " " + s2  # "hello world"

examples.output: Length: 3, characters: 'c' at index 0, 'a' at index 1, 't' at index 2
examples.trace:
String "cat" is a sequence of 3 characters.
Index 0: character 'c'
Index 1: character 'a'
Index 2: character 't'
Length is 3.

Empty string "" has no characters.
Length is 0.
No indices to access.

approaches.name: Rolling Hash with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the possible prefix lengths combined with rolling hash (Rabin-Karp) to quickly check if a prefix of given length appears elsewhere in the string. The binary search finds the maximum length satisfying the condition.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Prefix Matching Any Suffix with Rolling Hash and Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete O(n log n) solution using binary search on prefix lengths and rolling hash to check substring occurrences.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks: given a string s of length n, find the longest prefix of s that appears as a substring somewhere else in s (not necessarily as a suffix). The naive approach would check all prefixes against all substrings, giving O(n³) time. Our optimized approach uses two key ideas: 1) Binary search on the answer length L (0 to n-1), since if a prefix of length L works, any shorter prefix also works (monotonic property). 2) Rolling hash (Rabin-Karp) to quickly check if a prefix of length L appears elsewhere in s in O(n) time per check. The algorithm: precompute rolling hash values for all prefixes of s. For each candidate length L in binary search, compute hash of prefix s[0:L]. Then slide a window of length L through s[1:] (starting from index 1 to avoid trivial match at position 0), compute rolling hash of each substring s[i:i+L], and compare with prefix hash. If any match found, L is feasible; search higher lengths. Else, search lower. Time: O(n log n) — binary search O(log n) iterations, each O(n) hash computations. Space: O(n) for hash array. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: forgetting modulo arithmetic in hash, off-by-one in binary search bounds, not handling hash collisions (use double hash if needed). Real code pattern: precompute powers and prefix hashes, binary search with check function using rolling hash comparison.

examples.trace: String: 'ababa', n=5. Binary search L in [0,4]. Mid L=2: prefix 'ab' hash=hash('ab'). Check substrings of length 2 starting at indices 1-3: 'ba', 'ab', 'ba'. 'ab' at index 2 matches prefix → feasible. Search higher L=3: prefix 'aba' hash=hash('aba'). Check substrings length 3 at indices 1-2: 'bab', 'aba'. 'aba' at index 2 matches → feasible. Search higher L=4: prefix 'abab' hash=hash('abab'). Check substring length 4 at index 1: 'baba' no match → not feasible. Binary search ends with L=3 as max feasible. Output 3.

String: 'aaaaa', n=5. Binary search L in [0,4]. Mid L=2: prefix 'aa' matches everywhere → feasible. L=3: prefix 'aaa' matches at indices 1,2 → feasible. L=4: prefix 'aaaa' matches at index 1 → feasible. L=5 not considered (must be <n). Max L=4. Output 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rolling Hash Check for Prefix Occurrence
ladders.desc: Function that checks if a given prefix length L appears as a substring elsewhere in the string using rolling hash.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core check used in binary search. Given string s, length n, and candidate prefix length L, it determines if the prefix s[0:L] appears as a substring starting at any index i where 1 ≤ i ≤ n-L (avoiding the trivial match at i=0). We use rolling hash for efficiency. Steps: 1) Precompute: choose a large prime modulus (e.g., 10^9+7) and base (e.g., 26 for lowercase letters). Compute prefix hashes: H[i] = hash of s[0:i] using formula H[i] = (H[i-1]*base + val(s[i-1])) % mod. Also precompute powers: pow[i] = base^i % mod. 2) Compute hash of prefix: hash_pref = H[L]. 3) For each i from 1 to n-L: compute hash of substring s[i:i+L] using rolling hash: hash_sub = (H[i+L] - H[i]*pow[L]) % mod. Adjust to positive if negative. 4) If hash_sub equals hash_pref, return True (found match). After loop, return False. Time: O(n) per call. Space: O(1) extra, but relies on precomputed H and pow arrays (O(n) overall). Connection: This function is called in binary search to test each candidate L. Intuition: rolling hash allows O(1) substring hash computation after O(n) preprocessing. Common mistakes: incorrect modulo handling leading to negative values, not using same base/mod consistently, off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: implement check(L, s, H, pow, mod) that loops i and compares hashes.

examples.trace: Precomputed: base=26, mod=1e9+7, H=[0,1,27,703,18279,475255], pow=[1,26,676,...]. hash_pref = H[2]=27. Check i=1: substring 'ba', hash_sub = (H[3]-H[1]*pow[2])%mod = (703-1*676)%mod=27, matches → return True.
hash_pref = H[3]=hash('abc'). Check i=1: 'bcd' hash ≠. i=2: 'cde' hash ≠. No matches → return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Monotonic Length Property
ladders.desc: Using binary search to find maximum prefix length that appears elsewhere, leveraging monotonic feasibility.
ladders.explanation: The key insight: if a prefix of length L appears as a substring elsewhere in s, then any shorter prefix (length < L) also appears (because its substring is contained within the longer match or trivially). This monotonic property allows binary search. We define feasible(L) = True if prefix of length L appears elsewhere. Since feasible(L) is True for small L and False for large L (up to n-1), we can binary search for the maximum L where feasible(L) is True. Algorithm: initialize low=0, high=n-1 (since length must be < n). While low ≤ high: mid = (low+high)//2. If feasible(mid) is True (using rolling hash check), then search higher: low = mid+1. Else, search lower: high = mid-1. After loop, answer = high (the last True length). Time: O(log n) iterations, each calling O(n) check → O(n log n) total. Space: O(1) for binary search variables. Connection: This reduces problem from checking all lengths to logarithmic checks. Intuition: binary search works because feasibility function is monotonic—imagine gradually increasing L until it fails. Common mistakes: incorrect bounds (e.g., high=n), off-by-one in updating low/high, not handling edge cases like n=1. Real code pattern: standard binary search loop with check(mid) condition.

examples.trace: low=0, high=4. Mid=2: feasible(2)=True → low=3. Mid=3: feasible(3)=True → low=4. Mid=4: feasible(4)=False → high=3. low=4 > high=3, exit. Answer=high=3.
low=0, high=4. Mid=2: feasible(2)=False → high=1. Mid=0: feasible(0)=True (empty string trivially) → low=1. Mid=1: feasible(1)=False → high=0. low=1 > high=0, exit. Answer=high=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Rolling Hash (Rabin-Karp) for Substring Hashing
ladders.desc: Compute hash of any substring in O(1) after O(n) preprocessing using polynomial rolling hash.
ladders.explanation: Rolling hash is a technique to efficiently compute hash values of substrings by treating string as a polynomial. For string s of length n, choose a base b (e.g., 26 for lowercase letters) and modulus m (large prime, e.g., 10^9+7). Define hash of substring s[l:r] (0-indexed, inclusive l, exclusive r) as: hash = (s[l]*b^(r-l-1) + s[l+1]*b^(r-l-2) + ... + s[r-1]) % m, where s[i] is character code (e.g., a=1, b=2,...). Precompute: 1) prefix hash array H where H[i] = hash of s[0:i] (H[0]=0). Compute iteratively: H[i] = (H[i-1]*b + val(s[i-1])) % m. 2) power array pow where pow[i] = b^i % m. Then hash of s[l:r] = (H[r] - H[l]*pow[r-l]) % m. Adjust to positive if negative. This works because H[r] includes terms up to s[r-1], and subtracting H[l]*pow[r-l] removes terms before index l. Time: O(n) preprocessing, O(1) per substring query. Space: O(n) for H and pow. Connection: Used in check function to get substring hashes quickly. Intuition: polynomial representation allows subtraction to shift out unwanted prefix. Common mistakes: integer overflow (use modulo), base too small causing collisions, incorrect power computation. Real code pattern: precompute H and pow arrays, then hash(l,r) function.

examples.trace: H[5]=hash('ababa'), H[2]=hash('ab'), pow[3]=26^3%mod. hash = (H[5]-H[2]*pow[3])%mod. Compute to match hash('aba').

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Polynomial Hash of a String
ladders.desc: Compute hash value of a string using polynomial rolling hash formula.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental hashing operation: given a string str, compute its hash as an integer using the formula: hash = (str[0]*b^(k-1) + str[1]*b^(k-2) + ... + str[k-1]) % m, where k is length of str, b is base, m is modulus, and str[i] is integer value of character (e.g., 'a'=1, 'b'=2,... for lowercase). This treats the string as a polynomial with coefficients as character values, evaluated at base b, then taken modulo m to keep numbers manageable. Steps: initialize hash=0. For each character c in str: update hash = (hash * b + val(c)) % m. This iterative method avoids computing large powers directly. Time: O(k) for string of length k. Space: O(1). Connection: Used to compute initial prefix hashes in preprocessing. Intuition: each multiplication by b shifts previous hash left, adding new character as least significant digit. Common mistakes: not using modulo each step (causing overflow), choosing poor base/modulus (should be large prime to reduce collisions), incorrect character mapping. Real code pattern: function hash_string(s, base, mod) that loops through characters.

examples.trace: val(a)=1, val(b)=2. Start hash=0. Process 'a': hash = (0*26 + 1)%1009 = 1. Process 'b': hash = (1*26 + 2)%1009 = 28. Wait, correct: 1*26+2=28, but earlier example used 27? Let's recompute carefully: Actually, formula hash = s[0]*b^(1) + s[1]*b^(0) = 1*26 + 2 = 28. So output 28. My earlier trace had error; correcting here.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Prefix and Substring Definitions
ladders.desc: Understand what prefixes and substrings are, and how to identify them in a string.
ladders.explanation: A string is a sequence of characters. For a string s of length n (indices 0 to n-1): A prefix of s is any substring that starts at index 0. Formally, prefix of length L is s[0:L] (characters from index 0 to L-1). For example, in s='hello', prefixes are '' (empty, L=0), 'h' (L=1), 'he' (L=2), 'hel' (L=3), 'hell' (L=4), 'hello' (L=5). A substring is any contiguous sequence of characters from s. Formally, substring from index i to j-1 is s[i:j] where 0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n. For example, in 'hello', substrings include 'h', 'he', 'el', 'llo', 'hello', etc. The problem asks: find the longest prefix that appears as a substring somewhere else in s, meaning there exists some index i ≥ 1 such that s[i:i+L] equals s[0:L]. Note: it must appear elsewhere, not at position 0 (trivial). This is a basic string operation. To solve, we need to check each possible prefix length L from 0 to n-1, and for each, see if that prefix string matches any substring starting at i≥1. Time: naive O(n³) if checking all. Connection: This foundational concept leads to need for optimization via hashing and binary search. Intuition: understanding prefixes and substrings is key to framing the problem. Common mistakes: confusing prefix with subsequence (which need not be contiguous), off-by-one in indices, forgetting empty string case. Real code pattern: use slicing s[0:L] to get prefix, and loop i to compare with s[i:i+L].

examples.trace: String 'ababa', length 5. Prefix of length 2: s[0:2]='ab'. Check substrings of length 2 starting at indices 1,2,3: s[1:3]='ba', s[2:4]='ab', s[3:5]='ba'. 'ab' matches at index 2. So prefix appears elsewhere.
Prefix 'abc'. Check substrings length 3 at indices 1,2: s[1:4]='bcd', s[2:5]='cde'. Neither matches 'abc'. So not found.

testCases.expected: prefix='ban', appears at index ? (no), wait check: 'ban' vs 'ana', 'nan', 'ana' — no match, so not appear. Actually, correct: 'ban' does not appear elsewhere in 'banana' (substrings: 'ana', 'nan', 'ana'). So false.
prefix='miss', appears at index ? 'issi' no, 'ssis' no, etc. Actually, 'miss' does not appear elsewhere; check later: 'issi', 'ssis', 'siss', 'issi', 'ssip', 'sipp', 'ippi'. No 'miss'. So false.

approaches.name: Z-Algorithm O(n)
approaches.summary: Apply the Z-algorithm to compute the Z-array, where Z[i] gives the length of the longest substring starting at i that matches the prefix. The answer is the maximum Z[i] value for i > 0.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Prefix Matching Any Suffix using Z-Algorithm
ladders.desc: Find the longest prefix of a string that appears as a substring elsewhere in the string using Z-algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the longest prefix of a string that appears as a substring anywhere else in the string (not necessarily starting at position 0). The Z-algorithm provides an elegant O(n) solution by computing the Z-array, where Z[i] represents the length of the longest substring starting at position i that matches the prefix of the string. For i > 0, Z[i] gives us the length of prefix matches starting at various positions. The maximum Z[i] value (for i > 0) directly gives us the answer. Intuition: The Z-algorithm efficiently computes all prefix matches throughout the string in linear time by maintaining a 'window' of known matches. We initialize Z[0] = 0 (or n, but typically 0 for this problem), then iterate through the string while maintaining left and right boundaries of the current matching window. At each position i, if i is within the current window, we can use previously computed values to avoid redundant comparisons. Otherwise, we compare characters from the start. The algorithm cleverly reuses information to achieve O(n) time. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize Z array of size n with Z[0] = 0. 2. Set left = right = 0. 3. For i from 1 to n-1: a. If i <= right, set Z[i] = min(right-i+1, Z[i-left]). b. While i+Z[i] < n and s[Z[i]] == s[i+Z[i]], increment Z[i]. c. If i+Z[i]-1 > right, update left = i, right = i+Z[i]-1. 4. Find max Z[i] for i > 0. Time: O(n) due to each character compared at most twice. Space: O(n) for Z array. Connection to L1: This uses the Z-algorithm subroutine directly. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle the i > 0 condition when finding max, incorrect window updates, or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def longest_prefix_match(s): n = len(s); Z = [0]*n; l = r = 0; for i in range(1,n): if i <= r: Z[i] = min(r-i+1, Z[i-l]); while i+Z[i] < n and s[Z[i]] == s[i+Z[i]]: Z[i] += 1; if i+Z[i]-1 > r: l = i; r = i+Z[i]-1; return max(Z[1:]) if n>1 else 0.

examples.trace: String: 'ababa', n=5. Initialize Z=[0,0,0,0,0], l=0, r=0. i=1: i>r, so Z[1]=0. Compare s[0]='a' with s[1]='b' -> no match, Z[1]=0. l,r unchanged. i=2: i>r, Z[2]=0. Compare s[0]='a' with s[2]='a' -> match, Z[2]=1. Compare s[1]='b' with s[3]='b' -> match, Z[2]=2. Compare s[2]='a' with s[4]='a' -> match, Z[2]=3. End of string. Update l=2, r=4. i=3: i<=r, so Z[3]=min(r-i+1=4-3+1=2, Z[i-l]=Z[3-2]=Z[1]=0)=0. While loop: compare s[0]='a' with s[3]='b' -> no match, Z[3]=0. l,r unchanged. i=4: i<=r, Z[4]=min(r-i+1=4-4+1=1, Z[i-l]=Z[4-2]=Z[2]=3)=1. While loop: compare s[1]='b' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z=[0,0,3,0,1]. Max Z[i] for i>0 is max(0,3,0,1)=3. Output 3.

String: 'aaaaa', n=5. Initialize Z=[0,0,0,0,0], l=0, r=0. i=1: i>r, Z[1]=0. Compare s[0]='a' with s[1]='a' -> match, Z[1]=1. Compare s[1]='a' with s[2]='a' -> match, Z[1]=2. Compare s[2]='a' with s[3]='a' -> match, Z[1]=3. Compare s[3]='a' with s[4]='a' -> match, Z[1]=4. End of string. Update l=1, r=4. i=2: i<=r, Z[2]=min(r-i+1=4-2+1=3, Z[i-l]=Z[2-1]=Z[1]=4)=3. While loop: compare s[3]='a' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z[2]=3. i=3: i<=r, Z[3]=min(r-i+1=4-3+1=2, Z[i-l]=Z[3-1]=Z[2]=3)=2. While loop: compare s[2]='a' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z[3]=2. i=4: i<=r, Z[4]=min(r-i+1=4-4+1=1, Z[i-l]=Z[4-1]=Z[3]=2)=1. While loop: compare s[1]='a' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z[4]=1. Z=[0,4,3,2,1]. Max Z[i] for i>0 is max(4,3,2,1)=4. Output 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Z-Algorithm Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement the Z-algorithm to compute the Z-array for a given string.
ladders.explanation: The Z-algorithm computes an array Z where Z[i] is the length of the longest substring starting at position i that matches the prefix of the string. This is the core subroutine for the full problem. Intuition: We want to avoid redundant character comparisons by leveraging previously computed matches. We maintain a window [l, r] representing the rightmost matching segment found so far. For each position i, if it lies within this window, we can initialize Z[i] using the corresponding value from the prefix (Z[i-l]), but we must bound it by the window size to avoid overcounting. Then we extend by comparing characters beyond the window. This ensures each character is compared at most twice, giving O(n) time. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize Z array of size n, set Z[0] = 0 (or n, but 0 for consistency). 2. Set l = r = 0. 3. For i from 1 to n-1: a. If i <= r, set Z[i] = min(r - i + 1, Z[i - l]). b. While i + Z[i] < n and s[Z[i]] == s[i + Z[i]], increment Z[i]. c. If i + Z[i] - 1 > r, update l = i, r = i + Z[i] - 1. Time: O(n) as each character comparison either extends r or is skipped via the window. Space: O(n) for Z array. Connection to L0: This subroutine directly computes the Z-array needed to solve the full problem. Common mistakes: Incorrect bounds in the min function, forgetting to update l and r, or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def z_algorithm(s): n = len(s); Z = [0]*n; l = r = 0; for i in range(1, n): if i <= r: Z[i] = min(r - i + 1, Z[i - l]); while i + Z[i] < n and s[Z[i]] == s[i + Z[i]]: Z[i] += 1; if i + Z[i] - 1 > r: l = i; r = i + Z[i] - 1; return Z.

examples.trace: String: 'ababa', n=5. Initialize Z=[0,0,0,0,0], l=0, r=0. i=1: i>r, so Z[1]=0. Compare s[0]='a' with s[1]='b' -> no match, Z[1]=0. l,r unchanged. i=2: i>r, Z[2]=0. Compare s[0]='a' with s[2]='a' -> match, Z[2]=1. Compare s[1]='b' with s[3]='b' -> match, Z[2]=2. Compare s[2]='a' with s[4]='a' -> match, Z[2]=3. End of string. Update l=2, r=4. i=3: i<=r, so Z[3]=min(r-i+1=4-3+1=2, Z[i-l]=Z[3-2]=Z[1]=0)=0. While loop: compare s[0]='a' with s[3]='b' -> no match, Z[3]=0. l,r unchanged. i=4: i<=r, Z[4]=min(r-i+1=4-4+1=1, Z[i-l]=Z[4-2]=Z[2]=3)=1. While loop: compare s[1]='b' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z=[0,0,3,0,1]. Output [0,0,3,0,1].

String: 'aaaaa', n=5. Initialize Z=[0,0,0,0,0], l=0, r=0. i=1: i>r, Z[1]=0. Compare s[0]='a' with s[1]='a' -> match, Z[1]=1. Compare s[1]='a' with s[2]='a' -> match, Z[1]=2. Compare s[2]='a' with s[3]='a' -> match, Z[1]=3. Compare s[3]='a' with s[4]='a' -> match, Z[1]=4. End of string. Update l=1, r=4. i=2: i<=r, Z[2]=min(r-i+1=4-2+1=3, Z[i-l]=Z[2-1]=Z[1]=4)=3. While loop: compare s[3]='a' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z[2]=3. i=3: i<=r, Z[3]=min(r-i+1=4-3+1=2, Z[i-l]=Z[3-1]=Z[2]=3)=2. While loop: compare s[2]='a' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z[3]=2. i=4: i<=r, Z[4]=min(r-i+1=4-4+1=1, Z[i-l]=Z[4-1]=Z[3]=2)=1. While loop: compare s[1]='a' with s[5] out of bounds -> stop. Z[4]=1. Z=[0,4,3,2,1]. Output [0,4,3,2,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining a Matching Window for Efficient Comparisons
ladders.desc: Learn to maintain a sliding window [l, r] to reuse previously computed matches and avoid redundant comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves maintaining a window [l, r] that represents the rightmost segment where we have found a match with the prefix. The key insight is that if the current position i lies within this window, we can infer some information about Z[i] without comparing characters from scratch. Specifically, the substring starting at i within the window corresponds to a substring starting at i-l in the prefix (due to the match). Therefore, Z[i] can be at least as large as Z[i-l], but we must bound it by the remaining window size (r-i+1) to avoid exceeding the known match. This allows us to skip comparisons for characters already known to match. After initializing Z[i], we extend by comparing characters beyond the window. This technique ensures linear time complexity. Intuition: Think of the window as a 'known match region' that propagates information forward. When we compute Z[i], if i is inside the window, we borrow from earlier computations; otherwise, we start fresh. This dynamic updating minimizes work. Step-by-step: 1. Start with l = r = 0. 2. For each i from 1 to n-1: a. If i <= r, set k = i - l. Then Z[i] = min(r - i + 1, Z[k]). b. Extend Z[i] by comparing s[Z[i]] with s[i + Z[i]] until mismatch or end of string. c. If i + Z[i] - 1 > r, update l = i and r = i + Z[i] - 1. Time: O(n) because r moves monotonically rightward, and each character comparison increases r or is skipped. Space: O(1) extra for l and r. Connection to L1: This logic is the heart of the Z-algorithm implementation. Common mistakes: Forgetting the min operation, incorrectly updating l and r, or mishandling edge cases when i == r. Real code pattern: l = r = 0; for i in range(1, n): if i <= r: Z[i] = min(r - i + 1, Z[i - l]); while i + Z[i] < n and s[Z[i]] == s[i + Z[i]]: Z[i] += 1; if i + Z[i] - 1 > r: l = i; r = i + Z[i] - 1.

examples.trace: Start: l=0, r=0. i=1: i>r, so compute Z[1] from scratch: compare s[0]='a' vs s[1]='b', mismatch, Z[1]=0. r not extended, l,r remain (0,0). i=2: i>r, compute from scratch: compare s[0]='a' vs s[2]='a', match, Z[2]=1; compare s[1]='b' vs s[3]='b', match, Z[2]=2; compare s[2]='a' vs s[4]='a', match, Z[2]=3. Now i+Z[i]-1 = 2+3-1=4 > r=0, so update l=2, r=4. Window becomes (2,4). i=3: i=3 <= r=4, so use window: k=i-l=3-2=1, Z[3]=min(r-i+1=4-3+1=2, Z[k]=Z[1]=0)=0. Extend: compare s[0]='a' vs s[3]='b', mismatch, Z[3]=0. i+Z[i]-1=3+0-1=2 <= r=4, so no update. Window remains (2,4). i=4: i=4 <= r=4, use window: k=i-l=4-2=2, Z[4]=min(r-i+1=4-4+1=1, Z[k]=Z[2]=3)=1. Extend: i+Z[i]=4+1=5 out of bounds, so stop. Z[4]=1. i+Z[i]-1=4+1-1=4 <= r=4, no update. Final window (2,4).

Start: l=0, r=0. i=1: i>r, compute from scratch: compare s[0]='a' vs s[1]='a', match, Z[1]=1; compare s[1]='a' vs s[2]='a', match, Z[1]=2; compare s[2]='a' vs s[3]='a', match, Z[1]=3; compare s[3]='a' vs s[4]='a', match, Z[1]=4. i+Z[i]-1=1+4-1=4 > r=0, update l=1, r=4. Window (1,4). i=2: i=2 <= r=4, use window: k=i-l=2-1=1, Z[2]=min(r-i+1=4-2+1=3, Z[k]=Z[1]=4)=3. Extend: i+Z[i]=2+3=5 out of bounds, stop. Z[2]=3. i+Z[i]-1=2+3-1=4 <= r=4, no update. Window remains (1,4). i=3: i=3 <= r=4, k=3-1=2, Z[3]=min(r-i+1=4-3+1=2, Z[k]=Z[2]=3)=2. Extend: i+Z[i]=3+2=5 out of bounds, stop. Z[3]=2. No update. i=4: i=4 <= r=4, k=4-1=3, Z[4]=min(r-i+1=4-4+1=1, Z[k]=Z[3]=2)=1. Extend: i+Z[i]=4+1=5 out of bounds, stop. Z[4]=1. No update. Final window (1,4).

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Prefix Matches at a Given Position
ladders.desc: Given a string and a starting index i, compute the length of the longest substring starting at i that matches the prefix.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves computing, for a fixed position i in a string s, the length of the longest substring that starts at i and matches the prefix of s (i.e., the substring starting at index 0). This is essentially computing Z[i] manually without the window optimization. Intuition: We compare characters sequentially from the start of the string and from position i until we find a mismatch or reach the end. This brute-force approach helps understand what Z[i] represents before optimizing. It's a fundamental step that the Z-algorithm later optimizes. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize length = 0. 2. While i + length < n and s[length] == s[i + length], increment length. 3. Return length. Time: O(n) in worst case for each i, but overall O(n²) if done for all i. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is the naive computation that the window logic optimizes by reusing previous results. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check bounds, off-by-one errors, or misunderstanding that we compare with the prefix from start, not from i. Real code pattern: def prefix_match_at_i(s, i): n = len(s); length = 0; while i + length < n and s[length] == s[i + length]: length += 1; return length.

examples.trace: String: 'ababa', n=5, i=2. Initialize length=0. Check i+length=2+0=2 <5 and s[0]='a' == s[2]='a' -> match, length=1. Check i+length=2+1=3 <5 and s[1]='b' == s[3]='b' -> match, length=2. Check i+length=2+2=4 <5 and s[2]='a' == s[4]='a' -> match, length=3. Check i+length=2+3=5 not <5, stop. Return 3.

String: 'aaaaa', n=5, i=1. Initialize length=0. Check i+length=1+0=1 <5 and s[0]='a' == s[1]='a' -> match, length=1. Check i+length=1+1=2 <5 and s[1]='a' == s[2]='a' -> match, length=2. Check i+length=1+2=3 <5 and s[2]='a' == s[3]='a' -> match, length=3. Check i+length=1+3=4 <5 and s[3]='a' == s[4]='a' -> match, length=4. Check i+length=1+4=5 not <5, stop. Return 4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Substrings for Equality
ladders.desc: Given two indices in a string, compare substrings starting at those indices for equality up to a certain length.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two substrings within the same string to check if they are equal. Specifically, given a string s and indices i and j, we want to compare the substring starting at i with the substring starting at j, character by character, until a mismatch is found or we reach a specified limit. This is a fundamental operation used in string algorithms like the Z-algorithm. Intuition: We iterate through the characters, comparing s[i + k] with s[j + k] for k from 0 upwards, stopping when characters differ or when we exceed the string bounds. This simulates checking if two segments of the string match. It's a core building block for more complex pattern matching. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize k = 0. 2. While i + k < n and j + k < n and s[i + k] == s[j + k], increment k. 3. Return k (the length of match) or a boolean if only equality check is needed. Time: O(min(n-i, n-j)) in worst case. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly in computing prefix matches at a given position. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check array bounds, off-by-one errors, or confusing indices. Real code pattern: def compare_substrings(s, i, j): n = len(s); k = 0; while i + k < n and j + k < n and s[i + k] == s[j + k]: k += 1; return k.

examples.trace: String: 'hello', n=5, i=1, j=3. k=0: i+k=1, j+k=3, s[1]='e' != s[3]='l', mismatch, stop. Return 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding String Prefixes and Substrings
ladders.desc: Learn basic string concepts: prefixes, substrings, and how to access characters by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers essential string concepts needed for the problem. A string is a sequence of characters. A prefix is a substring that starts at the first character (index 0). For example, in string 'abc', prefixes are '', 'a', 'ab', 'abc'. A substring is any contiguous sequence of characters within the string. For 'abc', substrings include 'a', 'b', 'c', 'ab', 'bc', 'abc'. We access characters using indices starting from 0. Intuition: Understanding these definitions is crucial because the problem asks for a prefix that appears as a substring elsewhere. We need to compare parts of the string by looking at individual characters. Step-by-step: 1. Define a string s of length n. 2. Prefix: s[0:k] for k from 0 to n. 3. Substring: s[i:j] for 0 <= i <= j <= n. 4. Access character at index i: s[i]. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) for storing the string. Connection to L4: This knowledge allows us to perform substring comparisons. Common mistakes: Confusing prefixes with substrings, off-by-one errors in indices, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: In Python, s = 'hello'; prefix = s[:3] gives 'hel'; substring = s[1:4] gives 'ell'; char = s[0] gives 'h'.

examples.trace: String: 'apple', length 5. Prefixes: s[0:0]='', s[0:1]='a', s[0:2]='ap', s[0:3]='app', s[0:4]='appl', s[0:5]='apple'. Some substrings: s[1:3]='pp', s[2:5]='ple', s[0:2]='ap', etc. Character at index 0: s[0]='a'; index 1: s[1]='p'; index 2: s[2]='p'; index 3: s[3]='l'; index 4: s[4]='e'.

String: 'cat', length 3. Prefixes: s[0:0]='', s[0:1]='c', s[0:2]='ca', s[0:3]='cat'. Some substrings: s[1:3]='at', s[2:3]='t', s[0:1]='c'. Character at index 0: s[0]='c'; index 1: s[1]='a'; index 2: s[2]='t'.

approaches.name: KMP Prefix Function O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute the KMP prefix function (failure function) for the string, then find the maximum value in the prefix function array since it represents the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix at each position.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Prefix Matching Any Suffix Using KMP Prefix Function
ladders.desc: Find the longest prefix of a string that appears again as a substring anywhere in the string using KMP prefix function.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the longest prefix of a string that appears as a substring anywhere in the string (not necessarily at the end). The KMP prefix function (also called failure function) provides an elegant O(n) solution. For a string s of length n, the prefix function π[i] stores the length of the longest proper prefix of s[0..i] that is also a suffix of s[0..i]. The key insight is that the maximum value in the prefix function array gives us exactly what we need: the longest prefix that appears as a suffix of some prefix, which means it appears somewhere in the string. The algorithm works by computing the prefix function using the standard KMP approach, then finding the maximum value in the π array. The intuition is that at each position i, π[i] tells us how far we can match the beginning of the string against the suffix ending at i. By tracking the maximum of these values, we find the longest prefix that successfully matched somewhere in the string. The time complexity is O(n) since we compute the prefix function in linear time and find the maximum in O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing the prefix function array. Common mistakes include forgetting that we need the maximum value (not just π[n-1]), misunderstanding that we're looking for any substring (not just suffix), and off-by-one errors in the prefix function computation. This connects to L1 by using the prefix function computation as a key subroutine.

examples.trace:
String: ababab
Compute prefix function π:
π[0] = 0 (first character)
For i=1: s[1]=b, compare with s[0]=a, no match, π[1]=0
For i=2: s[2]=a, compare with s[0]=a, match, π[2]=1
For i=3: s[3]=b, compare with s[1]=b (since π[2]=1), match, π[3]=2
For i=4: s[4]=a, compare with s[2]=a (since π[3]=2), match, π[4]=3
For i=5: s[5]=b, compare with s[3]=b (since π[4]=3), match, π[5]=4
π array: [0,0,1,2,3,4]
Maximum value: 4
Longest prefix matching any suffix: 'abab' (length 4)

String: abcabcd
Compute prefix function π:
π[0]=0
i=1: s[1]=b vs s[0]=a, no match, π[1]=0
i=2: s[2]=c vs s[0]=a, no match, π[2]=0
i=3: s[3]=a vs s[0]=a, match, π[3]=1
i=4: s[4]=b vs s[1]=b (π[3]=1), match, π[4]=2
i=5: s[5]=c vs s[2]=c (π[4]=2), match, π[5]=3
i=6: s[6]=d vs s[3]=a (π[5]=3), no match, fall back to π[2]=0, compare s[6]=d vs s[0]=a, no match, π[6]=0
π array: [0,0,0,1,2,3,0]
Maximum value: 3
Longest prefix matching any suffix: 'abc' (length 3)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing the KMP Prefix Function
ladders.desc: Compute the prefix function (failure function) for a string, where π[i] = length of longest proper prefix of s[0..i] that is also a suffix.
ladders.explanation: The KMP prefix function is a fundamental array used in string matching algorithms. For each position i in string s (0-indexed), π[i] stores the length of the longest proper prefix of the substring s[0..i] that is also a suffix of that same substring. A proper prefix means it cannot be the entire substring. The algorithm builds this array incrementally using previously computed values. The intuition is that when we extend the string by one character, we try to extend the previous longest prefix-suffix match. If the new character matches the next character in the prefix, we can extend the match. If not, we fall back to the next best candidate using the prefix function itself—this is the clever recursive structure that makes it O(n). The algorithm: initialize π[0]=0. For i from 1 to n-1, set j=π[i-1]. While j>0 and s[i]≠s[j], set j=π[j-1]. If s[i]==s[j], then π[i]=j+1, else π[i]=0. The time complexity is O(n) because each character is processed a constant number of times (the while loop's total iterations are bounded by n). Space is O(n) for the π array. This connects to L0 as the core computation needed to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization, off-by-one errors in indices, and not understanding the fallback mechanism.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Understanding Prefix-Suffix Matching via Failure Function
ladders.desc: Understand how the prefix function identifies matching prefixes and suffixes at each position.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the KMP prefix function is the idea of matching a string against itself to find overlapping prefixes and suffixes. At each position i in the string, we want to know: what is the longest prefix of the entire string that ends at position i (or more precisely, that matches a suffix ending at i)? The prefix function π[i] gives us exactly this—the length of the longest proper prefix of s[0..i] that is also a suffix. The algorithm maintains a pointer j that represents the length of the current matching prefix. When we move to character s[i], we try to extend the match from position j. If s[i] matches s[j], we increment j. If not, we don't start over from the beginning; instead, we use the previously computed π values to fall back to the next best prefix that might match. This fallback works because π[j-1] tells us the longest prefix-suffix match for the prefix ending at j-1, which is essentially where we should try matching next. The intuition is similar to how we would slide a pattern in string matching. This mechanism ensures linear time complexity. This concept connects to L1 as it explains why the prefix function computation works. Common mistakes include misunderstanding what π[i] represents (it's for substring s[0..i], not the whole string) and not grasping the fallback logic.

examples.trace:
String: abab
At i=3 (0-indexed), we consider substring s[0..3]='abab'
We want longest proper prefix that is also a suffix.
Proper prefixes of 'abab': 'a', 'ab', 'aba'
Suffixes of 'abab': 'b', 'ab', 'bab', 'abab'
Matching prefix-suffix pairs: 'ab' (length 2) matches as both prefix and suffix.
'aba' doesn't match any suffix.
'a' matches but length 1 < 2.
So longest is 'ab' with length 2.
Thus π[3]=2.

String: aaa
At i=2, substring s[0..2]='aaa'
Proper prefixes: 'a', 'aa'
Suffixes: 'a', 'aa', 'aaa'
Matches: 'a' (length 1), 'aa' (length 2)
Longest is 'aa' with length 2.
Thus π[2]=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative String Comparison with Fallback
ladders.desc: Learn to compare a string against itself with intelligent fallback when mismatches occur.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the technique of comparing a string to itself to find overlaps, which is central to the prefix function. Given a string s, we want to find for each position i, how much of the beginning of the string matches the substring ending at i. We do this by maintaining two pointers: i iterates through the string, and j points to the next character to match in the prefix. Start with j=0. For each i from 1 to n-1: while j>0 and s[i]!=s[j], set j to π[j-1] (this is the fallback). Then if s[i]==s[j], increment j. Set π[i]=j. The fallback is crucial—it uses previously computed π values to jump to the next possible match without rechecking characters. The intuition is that if s[i] doesn't match s[j], instead of resetting j to 0, we check if a shorter prefix might match. Since π[j-1] tells us the longest prefix-suffix match for the prefix ending at j-1, we can continue matching from there. This ensures each character is processed O(1) times on average. This connects to L2 as it implements the core logic. Common mistakes include forgetting the while loop condition, incorrect fallback index, or not updating j correctly.

examples.input: Process character at i=3 in 'abac' with j=1
examples.trace:
String: abac
Assume we've processed up to i=2: π=[0,0,1], j=1 (from previous step)
At i=3: s[3]='c', s[j]=s[1]='b'
Since j=1>0 and 'c'!='b', we fallback: j=π[j-1]=π[0]=0
Now j=0, compare s[3]='c' with s[0]='a': no match
So π[3]=0, and j remains 0 for next iteration.

examples.input: Process character at i=4 in 'ababa' with j=2
examples.trace:
String: ababa
Assume π=[0,0,1,2], j=2 (from i=3)
At i=4: s[4]='a', s[j]=s[2]='a'
Since s[4]==s[2], we increment j to 3
Set π[4]=j=3
Now j=3 for potential next iteration.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character-by-Character String Comparison with Reset
ladders.desc: Compare two strings or substrings character by character, resetting on mismatch.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two sequences of characters element by element. Given two strings or indices into the same string, start from the beginning and compare each corresponding character. If all characters match up to a certain length, we have a match. If a mismatch occurs, we typically reset the comparison to start from the beginning of the pattern. For example, to check if a prefix of length k matches a substring ending at position i, we would compare s[0] with s[i-k+1], s[1] with s[i-k+2], ..., s[k-1] with s[i]. If any pair differs, the match fails. This is the naive approach that the KMP algorithm optimizes. The intuition is simple linear scanning. Time complexity for checking one potential match is O(k), and doing this naively for all positions would be O(n²). This operation connects to L3 as it's the fundamental comparison step that KMP makes more efficient with fallbacks. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, not handling empty strings, and incorrect reset logic.

examples.input: Check if prefix 'ab' matches substring ending at index 3 in 'abab'
examples.trace:
String: abab
Prefix: s[0..1]='ab'
Substring ending at i=3: s[2..3]='ab'
Compare s[0]='a' with s[2]='a': match
Compare s[1]='b' with s[3]='b': match
All characters match, so prefix 'ab' matches with length 2.

examples.input: Check if prefix 'abc' matches substring ending at index 4 in 'abcdabc'
examples.trace:
String: abcdabc
Prefix: s[0..2]='abc'
Substring ending at i=4: s[2..4]='cda'
Compare s[0]='a' with s[2]='c': mismatch
So match fails immediately, length 0.

testCases.input: Prefix 'a' vs substring at i=0 in 'a'
Prefix 'ab' vs substring at i=1 in 'ab'
Prefix 'aa' vs substring at i=2 in 'aaa'
Prefix 'xyz' vs substring at i=5 in 'xyzxyz'
Prefix 'cat' vs substring at i=4 in 'catdog'
Prefix 'hello' vs substring at i=9 in 'hellohello'
Prefix 'test' vs substring at i=3 in 'test'
Prefix 'ab' vs substring at i=3 in 'abca'
Prefix '123' vs substring at i=2 in '123'
Prefix 'x' vs substring at i=0 in 'y'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prefixes and Suffixes of Strings
ladders.desc: Understand what prefixes and suffixes are, and how to identify them in a string.
ladders.explanation: A prefix of a string is any substring that starts at the beginning of the string. For a string s of length n, the prefixes are s[0..0], s[0..1], s[0..2], ..., s[0..n-1]. A proper prefix is any prefix that is not the entire string. Similarly, a suffix is any substring that ends at the end of the string: s[0..n-1], s[1..n-1], ..., s[n-1..n-1]. A proper suffix is any suffix that is not the entire string. For example, for string 'apple', the prefixes are 'a', 'ap', 'app', 'appl', 'apple'. The proper prefixes are all except 'apple'. The suffixes are 'e', 'le', 'ple', 'pple', 'apple'. The proper suffixes are all except 'apple'. This concept is foundational because the KMP prefix function relies on comparing prefixes and suffixes. To work with them, you need to be able to extract substrings using indices. In programming, this is done with string slicing or loops. The intuition is that strings are sequences, and we can look at parts from the start (prefixes) or end (suffixes). This connects to L4 as it's the basic building block for comparison operations. Common mistakes include confusing prefixes with substrings (prefixes must start at index 0), forgetting about empty strings (usually not considered in this context), and off-by-one errors in indices.

examples.trace:
String: test (length 4)
Prefixes (starting at index 0):
- Length 1: s[0..0]='t'
- Length 2: s[0..1]='te'
- Length 3: s[0..2]='tes'
- Length 4: s[0..3]='test'
Suffixes (ending at index 3):
- Length 1: s[3..3]='t'
- Length 2: s[2..3]='st'
- Length 3: s[1..3]='est'
- Length 4: s[0..3]='test'

String: ab (length 2)
Prefixes:
- Length 1: 'a'
- Length 2: 'ab'
Suffixes:
- Length 1: 'b'
- Length 2: 'ab'
